claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 9 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask about your child:**

How old is the child (or children) you're claiming Child Benefit for? 

The age of your child determines the initial eligibility pathway:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** are not eligible

Please tell me the age of your child or children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children I'd like to claim for - Alex who is 9 years old, and Blake who is 5 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you with your Child Benefit eligibility for both Alex (9 years old) and Blake (5 years old).

Since both children are under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - both Alex and Blake are in the right age category. Now I need to check a few more requirements.

**Next question:**

Are both Alex (9) and Blake (5) working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?

(For children their age, this is typically not an issue, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not working at all - they're just kids! Alex is in primary school and Blake is as well.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now, another quick check:

**Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right** - such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Again, this is typically not applicable for young children like yours, but I need to confirm for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're too young for that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are either Alex or Blake in any type of apprenticeship program?

(Once again, at ages 9 and 5, this wouldn't apply, but I need to confirm this step.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - they're far too young for apprenticeships! They're still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Residency Requirements:**

To claim Child Benefit, you need to meet UK residency requirements. You can qualify through any of these paths:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, or
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or
3. You have pre-settled status and meet additional requirements (working, studying, jobseeking, or have sufficient resources), or
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you meet any of these residency requirements?** 

The most common situation is that you normally live in the UK. Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. We've lived here all our lives.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That clearly meets the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Responsibility for the children:**

To claim Child Benefit, you need to be responsible for Alex and Blake. You can qualify if:

1. **You live with the children**, or
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, housing, etc.), or
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex and Blake, or do you contribute to their upkeep in another way?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live at home with me. We all live together in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That clearly satisfies the responsibility requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about other claimants:**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake? For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A grandparent
- Any other person

If someone else is claiming, only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time. If both parties agree on who should claim, that's fine, but if there's a dispute, HMRC would need to decide.

**Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check the children's living situation:**

Are Alex or Blake currently:
- **In hospital** (for an extended period)?
- **In local authority care** (looked after by the council)?
- **Fostered** by you or someone else?
- Or are they **living normally at home** with you?

Which situation applies to your children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're just living normally at home with me - nothing out of the ordinary.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:**

Is the local council paying towards Alex or Blake's accommodation or maintenance costs?

(Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the council is paying for the child's upkeep.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. I'm covering all their costs myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me proceed to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You're Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result:** You meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for **both Alex and Blake**.

### **Eligibility Summary:**

✅ **For Alex (9 years old):** ELIGIBLE
- Under 16 years old
- Living with you in the UK
- Not working or receiving benefits
- No other competing claims
- All requirements met

✅ **For Blake (5 years old):** ELIGIBLE
- Under 16 years old
- Living with you in the UK
- Not working or receiving benefits
- No other competing claims
- All requirements met

---

### **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Blake

---

### **Important Information:**

📌 **Payments:** Child Benefit is paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge:** Be aware that if you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge through your tax return. However, you can still claim Child Benefit to protect National Insurance credits for your State Pension.

📋 **More information and how to apply:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester) and is responsible for Alex (lives with them). No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 5 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester) and is responsible for Blake (lives with them). No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children, Alex (9) and Blake (5). Both children are under 16 and living normally at home with the claimant in Manchester. The claimant meets all residency and responsibility requirements, with no other competing claims or disqualifying factors present for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester) and is responsible for Alex (lives with them). No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 5 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with the claimant in the UK, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester) and is responsible for Blake (lives with them). No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children, Alex (9) and Blake (5). Both children are under 16 and living normally at home with the claimant in Manchester. The claimant meets all residency and responsibility requirements, with no other competing claims or disqualifying factors present for either child.
